---
title: "1. Why CRTs?"
date-modified: last-modified
---

::: {.callout-note .draft}
**DISCLAIMER: This page is under construction**  
**Draft scaffold.** Body needs:
(1) ~3 paragraphs framing CRTs vs individually-randomized trials,
(2) the HIV prevention motivation [@balzer2021twostage],
(3) brief stats — 700+ active NIH CRTs, 80+ HIV-prevention CRTs,
(4) embedded short video (~2 min) from the SAB on why this matters,
(5) a "further reading" Resources panel matching the
[NIH Collaboratory Living Textbook](https://rethinkingclinicaltrials.org/)
look.
:::

## When you need a CRT

Some interventions act at the **group level** — village health teams,
clinic care-delivery models, public-health campaigns — and cannot be
delivered to an individual in isolation. For these, the only valid
randomized comparison randomizes *groups*. Examples:

- **Universal HIV test-and-treat** (SEARCH): 32 communities in Kenya and
  Uganda randomized to a community-level prevention package vs.
  standard-of-care.
- **Dynamic Choice HIV Prevention** (SAPPHIRE, Peer-led DCP): 16 and 14
  communities respectively, offering choice-based PrEP/PEP delivery.
- **Isoniazid preventive therapy** (SEARCH-IPT): district-randomized
  trial to improve IPT uptake.

CRTs preserve the rigor of randomization while letting researchers ask
questions that map directly to **how interventions are deployed in the
real world** [@balzer2021twostage]. They are increasingly the design of
choice for translating efficacy into effectiveness.

## What makes CRTs hard

Compared to individually-randomized trials, CRTs face three persistent
challenges:

### Few independent units

Most CRTs randomize **fewer than 40 clusters**. With small numbers of
independent units, the dependence among participants within a cluster
sharply *reduces the effective sample size*, and standard inferential
tools (mixed models, generalized estimating equations) tend to perform
poorly with finite-sample corrections.

### Missing outcomes

One review found **93% of CRTs had missing outcomes**. Naïve
complete-case analyses are biased when participants with measured
outcomes differ meaningfully from those with missed outcomes. The
problem is worse when the missingness mechanism is *itself influenced
by the intervention* — e.g., when the intervention's first action is to
extend screening.

### Effect heterogeneity

Fewer than 30% of CRTs assess effect heterogeneity, because practical
guidance on such analyses has been lacking
[@balzer2021twostage]. Established subgroups (sex, age group, region)
provide some insight, but miss the chance to identify multilevel factors
that predict who benefits most.

## Where this site fits

The remaining chapters build a single coherent methodology that
addresses all three challenges — anchored on the Causal Roadmap
([Chapter 2](02_info_roadmap.qmd)) and implemented in the
[`TwoStageTMLE`](https://github.com/LauraBalzer/TwoStageTMLE) R package.

## Resources

::: {.callout-tip}
**Further reading**

- David Murray's [NIH ODP online course on pragmatic and group-randomized
  trials](https://prevention.nih.gov/education-training/pragmatic-and-group-randomized-trials-public-health-and-medicine) [@murray2020pragmatic]
- The [NIH Collaboratory Rethinking Clinical Trials Living
  Textbook](https://rethinkingclinicaltrials.org/)
:::
